items.title: Sweep η on a log grid before long runs
items.avoid: Committing to a single η from a blog post for a multi-day training job.
items.prefer: Short runs (1–5 epochs) across η ∈ {1e-5, 3e-5, 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, …} and pick the best validation trend.
items.why: Log spacing covers orders of magnitude efficiently; early curves reveal divergence vs healthy descent.
items.example: Plot val loss vs step for η ∈ [1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3]; discard any curve that spikes upward in the first epoch.

items.title: Reduce η when loss oscillates or diverges
items.avoid: Adding gradient clipping alone while keeping an obviously too-large η.
items.prefer: Halve η (or apply step decay) and confirm smoother loss; combine clipping only if spikes persist after lr reduction.
items.why: Oscillation usually means steps exceed stable curvature bounds; clipping limits magnitude but does not fix wrong scale.
items.example: If train loss alternates 0.8 ↔ 1.2 every step at η=0.5, try η=0.05 before tuning other knobs.

items.title: Schedule η for late-stage fine convergence
items.avoid: Running constant η through entire training when early fast descent later turns into noisy plateau.
items.prefer: Use step decay, exponential decay, or cosine schedule after baseline constant-η experiments work.
items.why: Large η helps early; smaller η reduces overshoot near minima and stabilizes validation metrics.
items.example: SGD lr=0.1 for 30 epochs, then StepLR(step_size=10, gamma=0.1) drops to 0.01 for final polishing.
